build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Crime scenes have interactive hotspots that reveal evidence when clicked. Each piece of evidence is added to a notebook with its details (description, source, relevance). At least three hotspots per scene exist. Score 0 if there are no interactive hotspots, if clicking reveals nothing, or if no notebook exists. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A deduction board allows the player to connect evidence to suspects, timelines, and motives by dragging links between cards. Connections are persistent and form the basis for accusations. Score 0 if there is no deduction board, if connections cannot be made, or if evidence cannot be linked to suspects. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The player makes accusations by selecting who, what method, and when. A correct accusation solves the case with a reveal sequence. An incorrect accusation costs credibility points. Too many wrong guesses end the case in failure. Score 0 if accusations cannot be made, if there is no penalty for wrong guesses, or if the case cannot be solved. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Suspects can be interviewed with dialogue that reveals information. The player can press on statements to probe deeper, sometimes unlocking new evidence or contradictions. Score 0 if interviews do not exist, if pressing has no effect, or if dialogue reveals nothing useful. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

The full loop connects: title -> case select -> investigate (scene/interview) -> deduce (board) -> accuse -> result (solved/failed) -> return. Both success and failure are reachable. Score 0 if any transition is missing or if one outcome is unreachable. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least five evidence items exist per case with different types (physical, testimonial, documentary) and roles in the logic chain. Not all evidence is relevant — some are red herrings. Score 0 if fewer than three evidence items exist or if all evidence is equally relevant. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least three suspects exist with different motives, alibis, and relationships to the victim. Each suspect has a distinct visual appearance and personality. Score 0 if fewer than two suspects exist or if suspects are interchangeable. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Multiple locations can be visited (crime scene, suspect homes, public places) each with different visual settings and evidence to find. Score 0 if only one location exists or if locations have no unique content. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

The deduction requires genuine logical reasoning: evidence must be cross-referenced, alibis checked against timelines, and contradictions identified. The solution is not obvious from a single clue. Score 0 if the solution is trivially obvious or if no reasoning is required. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Both solved and failed case outcomes have case-specific text, summary of evidence found, and deduction accuracy rating. The result feels like a narrative conclusion, not a generic screen. Score 0 if outcomes are generic or if one outcome is missing. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Normal startup shows a styled title screen with noir atmosphere (rain, shadows, city silhouette) and case selection. Score 0 if it launches into raw gameplay or default UI. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The flow connects title -> case -> investigation -> deduction board -> accusation -> result without restarting. The player always knows what phase they are in and what actions are available. Score 0 if transitions are broken or if phases are indistinguishable. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Evidence details, suspect dialogue, deduction board connections, and credibility are readable at 1280x720 without overlap. The player can always parse available options. Score 0 if text is unreadable or if UI elements overlap critically. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Key moments have visible feedback: finding evidence shows a discovery effect, correct deductions show confirmation, wrong accusations show penalty, and the reveal sequence has dramatic staging. Score 0 if important events have no visual feedback. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Overall art coherence: crime scenes, characters, deduction board, UI, and effects belong to a consistent noir visual language with shadows, rain, and muted tones. Score 0 if the screen mixes incompatible styles or reads as plain default Godot grey. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Crime scene backgrounds and location art are real illustrated environments with atmospheric detail (shadows, rain streaks, dim lighting). Evidence items are illustrated objects, not plain text. Score 0 if scenes are flat colored backgrounds. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Character portraits are real illustrated or pixel-art figures with distinct appearances and expressions. Suspects look like noir characters (trench coats, fedoras, cigarette smoke). Score 0 if characters have no portraits or are plain shapes. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

UI and deduction board use styled noir chrome: evidence cards have case-file styling, the notebook has leather-bound aesthetics, and transitions use dramatic noir effects (fade to black, spotlight reveals). Score 0 if UI elements are unstyled Godot defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
